chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP backend is unreachable, so I cannot decompile decodeBytes or the JNI entry, but I still must produce an assessment from the available high-level info.

The crash is in strchr_default, which implies a call to strchr or a similar C string routine from decodeBytes. That means a pointer or string is invalid or not null-terminated; typical issues: passing attacker-controlled bytes without ensuring a terminator or valid bounds.

Stack: strchr_default <- decodeBytes <- Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse <- fuzz_one_input. This suggests decodeBytes is an internal helper taking the Java byte[] (XML or KML data) and converting/parsing it, probably treating it as a C string, calling strchr() to scan for delimiters or tags.

The JNI bridge method jniParse([B)J is called from TBXML.parse(String) in Java. The fuzzer harness fuzz_one_input likely feeds arbitrary bytes, not guaranteed to be null-terminated or valid UTF-8/ASCII. In a production app, TBXML.parse(String) will turn a Java String into bytes (likely UTF-8) and they will be well-formed but still arbitrary in contents, and Java arrays carry their length separately; C must not assume null termination.

If decodeBytes calls strchr() directly on the raw bytes from the JNI array without copying/appending a terminator or respecting the JNI-reported length, it invokes undefined behavior when there is no '\0' in-bounds, potentially causing out-of-bounds read. Even if allocator/ASan catches it as a crash in strchr_default, the underlying bug is unsafe use of C string APIs on untrusted data length.

Is this realistically attacker-controlled? TBXML.parse(String) is used by PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String), presumably loading KML content from app/network. KML files are external data (maps overlay). So an attacker controlling KML could influence the string passed to TBXML and thus the bytes passed to decodeBytes.

While the fuzzer may be using unrealistic arrays (missing null), the core pattern (no length check, C-string assumption) is still a logic-level bug. Out-of-bounds read via strchr is a memory safety issue; with certain conditions it might escalate, but at minimum it is a denial-of-service and possibly an info leak if other APIs are involved.

I cannot prove exact src/dst/len, but the presence of strchr_default and a decodeBytes helper between JNI and libc, combined with typical TBXML parsers (small C XML parser) that historically have had such bugs, makes it very likely that decodeBytes treats untrusted data as NUL-terminated without bounds.

Therefore I will classify as a likely real vulnerability: memory safety (out-of-bounds read) caused by misuse of C string APIs on JNI byte[]/String input. Severity: at least medium (DoS via crafted KML/XML), potentially high if deeper memory corruption is possible, but we only see strchr, so I choose medium. Confidence around 0.7 due to lack of direct code view.

Affected lib is clearly libtbxml.so; JNI entry is Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse; call_sequence is main -> fuzz_one_input -> Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse -> decodeBytes -> strchr_default.
reasons: Crash occurs in strchr_default, indicating unsafe C-string handling in decodeBytes within libtbxml.so.
JNI method jniParse([B)J passes attacker-influenced byte[] (KML/XML data) into native code without evidence of length-based validation.
Using strchr on raw JNI bytes without ensuring in-bounds NUL-termination can cause out-of-bounds reads and process crash (DoS).
recommendations: Review decodeBytes and all uses of strchr/str*/mem* in libtbxml.so to ensure they operate on bounded buffers using explicit lengths (e.g., memchr, strn* variants) or on copies with guaranteed NUL-termination.
Add robust input validation at the JNI layer (jniParse) to ensure that buffers passed into C obey expected invariants, or rework the API to pass explicit length instead of assuming C-strings.
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan enabled on a fixed build and expand coverage around TBXML parsing functions to catch similar unsafe string/memory operations.
assumptions: decodeBytes calls strchr (or wrapper) directly on bytes derived from the Java-provided buffer/string without separately tracking the buffer length.
TBXML.parse(String) can be reached with attacker-controlled KML/XML data from outside the app, as suggested by PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml usage.
limitations: Ghidra MCP backend was unreachable, so no direct decompilation of decodeBytes or JNI bridge was possible; reasoning is based solely on stack trace and typical TBXML usage patterns.
Exact nature of the fault inside strchr_default (pure OOB read vs more complex corruption) is unknown from the provided trace alone.

chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP and Jadx MCP are unreachable, so I must reason only from the symbolic stack, names, and description.

The crash is reported as a termination in strpbrk, a standard C library string-scanning function. Typical strpbrk crashes are due to passing it a non‑null‑terminated or otherwise invalid char* pointer or haystack, often from untrusted or malformed data.
The first app-level native frame below libc is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, then the JNI bridge Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse. The lib map confirms both live in the same app library.

Given the library name (libtbxml.so) and class TBXML, decodeBytes is almost certainly responsible for parsing / decoding a byte[] XML buffer coming from Java (jniParse([B)J), meaning the fuzzer-controlled byte[] is passed to native code and then to decodeBytes.

The Java call graph shows that in normal app use, jniParse([B)J is reached from TBXML.parse(String) via PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String), which likely loads KML/XML content (potentially from remote/network / user-supplied files). Such content is reasonably attacker-controlled in a maps/KML overlay plugin context.

A realistic misuse leading to a crash in strpbrk would be: decodeBytes iterates over XML text using strpbrk on a pointer derived from the untrusted buffer without enforcing proper length or null termination (e.g., treating raw bytes as C string). If the JNI/native code fails to ensure a terminating '\0' or bounds checking, strpbrk can read beyond the buffer until it hits unmapped memory, causing a crash (out-of-bounds read).

Even if the exact decompiled code is unavailable, the function names and signature strongly suggest such a pattern: a decodeBytes routine plus a standard C string function, operating on a Java-provided byte[]. There is no evidence of protective wrappers or checked length handling; direct use of strpbrk on untrusted buffers is unsafe if they are not guaranteed to be null‑terminated, and JNI often passes raw bytes and length separately.

The fuzzer harness confirms that fuzz_one_input leads directly into the JNI path, meaning the crash is reproducible with arbitrary byte[] data, reinforcing that there is a memory-safety issue in normal code (not just the harness passing impossible parameters such as NULL pointers).

Therefore, this is best classified as a likely out-of-bounds read / invalid pointer dereference in decodeBytes / its use of strpbrk, with attacker-controlled input via KML/XML data. This is at least a DoS (app crash on malformed input), and potentially could be escalated depending on allocator and surrounding logic; however, with only an out-of-bounds read in strpbrk, high-confidence RCE cannot be claimed.
Thus I classify it as a real vulnerability (memory safety, attacker-controlled buffer interpreted as C-string) with medium severity and moderately high confidence.
reasons: Crash occurs inside strpbrk called from app code decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, indicating unsafe use of C string primitives.

jniParse([B)J takes a Java byte[] buffer, which is then processed by decodeBytes without any visible guarantee of null termination or bounds checking before calling strpbrk, enabling out-of-bounds reads on attacker-controlled data.

Java call graph shows the JNI method is reachable via TBXML.parse and PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml, which process KML/XML content that can plausibly be influenced by external/untrusted sources, making this path realistic beyond the fuzz harness.

evidence.note: Crash in strpbrk when called from decodeBytes; typical cause is out-of-bounds read from non-null-terminated or otherwise invalid string pointer derived from untrusted buffer.
JNI bridge takes a byte[] and passes it to native code, which then calls decodeBytes and ultimately strpbrk.
Call graph shows KML/XML content flowing into TBXML.jniParse([B)J, making it likely attacker-controllable in real app scenarios.

recommendations: In decodeBytes, stop using strpbrk on raw buffers from Java; instead, track buffer length explicitly and use bounded functions (e.g., memchr/strcspn-style logic that never reads past end).

Ensure JNI layer either copies the Java byte[] into a newly allocated buffer with an explicit '\0' terminator or always passes both pointer and length and enforces bounds checks before any C-string operation.
Add robust input validation for KML/XML data and add defensive checks against malformed/unterminated data, plus regression tests with fuzzed inputs to prevent recurrence.
assumptions: decodeBytes operates on the byte[] passed from jniParse([B)J without always allocating a separate null-terminated copy.
The KML/XML content processed by PluginKmlOverlay and TBXML is at least partially attacker-controlled (e.g., from files, network, or external apps).
The crash corresponds to an out-of-bounds read in strpbrk rather than a purely internal libc bug.
limitations: Unable to decompile libtbxml.so or view exact implementation of decodeBytes and JNI bridge due to MCP connection issues.
No direct crash register/PC or memory map details to distinguish between simple invalid-pointer deref vs. large over-read.
Lack of Java source snippets to confirm exact origin and trust level of the KML/XML data.
